title: SQL Query Optimizer
description: Use when a SQL query, database endpoint, report, or job has slow runtime, high cost, poor plan shape, missing indexes, or needs safer query/index recommendations.

workload_classes.Point lookup/API path: High p95/p99 on selective lookup; prefer equality-first covering indexes and stable parameter plans.
workload_classes.Paginated list: Slow or unstable pages; prefer keyset pagination and deterministic filter/sort/id indexes.
workload_classes.Reporting/aggregation: Long scans or spills; prefer pre-aggregation, partition pruning, and group/filter-aware indexes.
workload_classes.Join-heavy query: Bad join order or row explosion; prefer join key indexes, stats, and pre-filtering.
workload_classes.Write-heavy table: Indexes slow writes; prefer partial/narrow indexes and query rewrites.
workload_classes.Multi-tenant workload: Tenant hot spots or cross-tenant scans; prefer tenant-leading indexes or partitioning.

bottleneck_types.Missing/unused index: Sequential scan on selective predicate or key lookup storm.
bottleneck_types.Non-sargable predicate: Function, cast, or wildcard prevents index use.
bottleneck_types.Cardinality mismatch: Estimated rows far from actual rows.

instructions: Identify database engine/version when available; do not assume PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, SQL Server, BigQuery, Snowflake, or other engine behavior without evidence.
Classify workload class and bottleneck type before recommending indexes.
Summarize query purpose, tables, joins, filters, grouping, ordering, limits, and expected row counts.
Inspect the plan for scan type, join order, row estimates vs actuals, sort/hash spill, temp files, key lookups, sequential scans, remote reads, partition pruning, and late filters.
Identify the dominant bottleneck with evidence such as estimated/actual rows, loops, timing, I/O, memory, cardinality mismatch, or missing statistics.
Compare index options when more than one is plausible, including column order, uniqueness/partial/covering choices, write cost, and which query clause each column serves.
Recommend rewrites only when they preserve semantics; call out NULL behavior, duplicates, time zone/date boundaries, collation, and aggregation changes.
Include trade-offs such as write amplification, lock/build strategy, storage, planner risk, stale stats, and migration/rollback plan.
Define validation with before/after EXPLAIN ANALYZE, representative parameters, cache caveats, runtime budget, and regression checks.

examples.input: Query on orders table is slow with full table scan.
examples.output: Likely Bottleneck: full scan on orders because the filter cannot use an index. Recommended Index: (customer_id, created_at) if equality on customer_id and range/order on created_at are the dominant predicates. Query Rewrite: replace function-wrapped date filter with a half-open range predicate to preserve index use. Trade-offs: extra write cost and storage on orders. Validation Plan: run before/after EXPLAIN ANALYZE with representative customer IDs and check that related write-heavy paths stay within budget.
